title: The flow of arms and money feeding the war in Sudan can be cut. What is missing is the will
gist: Chatham House argues Sudan's civil war between the SAF and RSF is sustained mainly by external arms, financing and logistics networks spanning the UAE, Turkey, Iran, Egypt, Eritrea, Ethiopia, Libya, Chad and Kenya, and that ending it requires US-led 'deproxification' - disrupting these flows - which Washington has the leverage but not yet the political will to pursue.

insights.claim: Sudan's war between the Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF) and Rapid Support Forces (RSF) persists chiefly because non-Sudanese actors supply externally procured weapons and cross-border logistics that sustain both sides' battlefield capacity, making continued fighting look more rational to them than a negotiated exit.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Reframes Sudan as a proxy-war problem, meaning peace depends on cutting external supply chains, not just internal mediation.
insights.quote: Diplomacy that does not disrupt the flow of foreign weapons, finance, and logistics into Sudan is underwriting, rather than ending the war.

insights.claim: Sudan's three-year war between the SAF and RSF has produced what the UN calls the world's worst humanitarian crisis, displacing 14 million people.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Scale of displacement signals prolonged regional instability risk and humanitarian funding needs across East Africa.

insights.claim: The April 2026 Berlin Conference delivered €1.5 billion in humanitarian pledges and produced the Berlin Principles for Sudan - the most explicit multilateral call yet for external backers to halt support to the SAF and RSF - but it did not rule out the warring parties controlling Sudan's transition, did not name their external backers, and imposed no real costs on enablers.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Humanitarian pledges without enforcement mechanisms will not change the war's trajectory; watch for whether future conferences name and sanction backers.

insights.claim: The Quad (US, Egypt, Saudi Arabia, UAE) tasked with ending the war is structurally dysfunctional because two of its own members, the UAE and Egypt, are accused of enabling Sudan's warring parties while being expected to lead ceasefire efforts; its September roadmap had no enforcement mechanism and was rejected by the SAF before it could be tested.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A mediating body with a built-in conflict of interest is unlikely to produce enforceable outcomes; investors should discount near-term ceasefire probability via Quad channels.

insights.claim: A Wall Street Journal investigation, Amnesty International field documentation, and UN expert reporting all allege the UAE has transferred arms, including advanced Chinese drone systems, to the RSF; Abu Dhabi denies this and has faced no formal censure.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: UAE is identified as the single most consequential and most-documented external enabler of the RSF, making it the key target for any future sanctions leverage.

insights.claim: Turkey reportedly supplied Bayraktar-type drone systems to the SAF at virtually no diplomatic cost (Turkey denies direct supply), and Iran is also alleged to have supplied drones to the SAF (which the SAF denies).
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Multiple state suppliers on the SAF side, alongside the UAE-RSF pipeline, show the conflict is a genuine multi-party proxy war rather than a two-actor conflict.

insights.claim: Drone strikes account for over 80% of at least 880 documented civilian deaths in Sudan between January and April 2026 alone.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Quantifies how foreign-supplied drone technology, not just small arms, is now the dominant driver of civilian casualties, sharpening the case for targeting drone supply chains specifically.

insights.claim: Egypt publicly champions Sudan's territorial integrity while materially supporting the SAF, including integrating commodity networks into the SAF war economy, and has reportedly established a drone base in the Oweinat tri-border area with Libya and Sudan, signaling deepening entanglement in the conflict.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Egypt's dual posture (public neutrality, private backing) undermines its credibility as a Quad mediator.

insights.claim: Eritrea has reportedly hosted and trained pro-SAF militia in Eastern Sudan, helping the SAF retake central Sudan and Khartoum from the RSF, while Ethiopia has reportedly allowed its territory to be used to train RSF fighters; this month the SAF accused Ethiopia and the UAE of a drone strike on Khartoum's international airport, which both countries deny.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Shows the conflict's external-backer roster is widening rather than narrowing, complicating any single-track sanctions or diplomatic strategy.

insights.claim: Ethiopia and Uganda, both accused by the SAF of enabling opposing sides in the war (Uganda's President Museveni met RSF commander 'Hemedti' this year), sit together on the African Union's Peace and Security Council, making that body structurally incapable of holding either party accountable.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Signals the AU cannot be relied upon as an independent accountability mechanism for Sudan, pointing responsibility back toward US/Western sanctions tools.

insights.claim: The UN confirms Libyan territory is used to facilitate cross-border movement of fighters, arms and materiel to the RSF, and identifies similar supply lines running through Chad; Kenya hosted RSF representatives in Nairobi early this year who announced the formation of a parallel government, which Kenyan President William Ruto denies supporting.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A wide arc of transit and hosting states (Libya, Chad, Kenya) means enforcement requires coordinated multilateral pressure, not bilateral deals with any single neighbor.

insights.claim: Chatham House has documented gold as the war's 'connective tissue,' leaving Sudan through informal corridors across East Africa with South Sudan serving as a key RSF logistics node, giving the conflict a self-sustaining business logic independent of any single patron.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Gold-smuggling corridors are a concrete, trackable target for sanctions and commodity-traceability enforcement aimed at defunding the RSF.

insights.claim: What Sudan needs is 'deproxification' - ending the process by which external actors fuel the war with the SAF and RSF as their proxies - which requires coordinated disruption of every arms route, gold shipment, and logistics corridor sustaining the conflict.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete policy framework investors and NGOs can use to evaluate whether future peace initiatives are substantive or merely symbolic.

insights.claim: The US holds the greatest leverage to pressure Sudan's war enablers - particularly through sanctions on the UAE for its RSF patronage - but has been unwilling to use it because it views strong UAE relations as essential to its Gaza, Iran, and Abraham Accords agenda.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: Highlights a direct policy trade-off: US Middle East diplomatic priorities are effectively subsidizing the Sudan war by shielding the UAE from sanctions.
insights.quote: The US holds the greatest leverage but has yet to show the willingness to use it.

insights.claim: The Trump administration should consider handing the Sudan file to Vice President JD Vance, since prior envoys Tom Perriello and Massad Boulos carried insufficient weight in Abu Dhabi, whereas Vance's back-channel diplomacy experience on Iran and his authority to coordinate the Treasury, State Department and other agencies could make US pressure more credible.
insights.speaker: Chatham House
insights.so_what: A concrete personnel signal to watch: if Vance is given the Sudan file, it would indicate the US is finally prepared to escalate pressure on the UAE.
insights.quote: The Trump administration should seriously consider handing the Sudan file to Vice President JD Vance.
